content.problemStatement:
Alice has an array AAA of length NNN which is initially a permutation. She dislikes KKK numbers which are B1,B2,…,BKB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_KB1​,B2​,…,BK​ all of which are **distinct**. Therefore, she removes all the occurrences of these numbers from AAA. The order of the remaining elements of the AAA does **not** change.

Can you find out the resulting array AAA?

Note: A permutation of length NNN is an array where every integer from 111 to NNN occurs exactly once.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.
The third line of each test case contains an integer KKK — the size of the array BBB.
The fourth line of each test case contains KKK integers B1,B2,…,BKB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_KB1​,B2​,…,BK​ denoting the numbers which Alice dislikes.


Output Format
For each test case, output the resulting array AAA after the removal of all occurrences of B1,B2,…BKB_1, B_2, \ldots B_KB1​,B2​,…BK​.


It is guaranteed that there will be at least one element in the resulting array.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 200000
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- A line with integer N, the size of the permutation.
- A line with N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, the permutation.
- A line with integer K, the number of disliked numbers.
- A line with K space‑separated distinct integers B1 … BK.
It is guaranteed that the resulting array is non‑empty.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the elements of the array after removing all occurrences of the disliked numbers, preserving their original order. Elements are separated by a single space.

hidden.label: edge case – single disliked element in middle

solution.approach:
For each test case we store all disliked numbers in a hash set (or a boolean marker array). Then we iterate through the original permutation and output each element that is not present in the set. This preserves the original order and runs in linear time.
Steps:
1. Read T.
2. Loop over test cases:
   a. Read N and the permutation A.
   b. Read K and the list B.
   c. Insert every B[i] into a set (or mark in a boolean array of size N+1).
   d. Scan A; for each value x, if x is not in the set, append it to the answer.
   e. Print the answer line.
The total work is O(total N) and the extra memory is O(K) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(Σ N) – each element of each test case is processed a constant number of times.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(K) per test case for the set of disliked numbers (at most O(N)).

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().strip().split()))
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = next(it)
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = next(it)
        A = [next(it) for _ in range(N)]
        K = next(it)
        bad = set(next(it) for _ in range(K))
        res = [str(x) for x in A if x not in bad]
        out_lines.append(' '.join(res))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
